title: Nail Polish on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes home triage; acetone risk on polyester is real, hidden test mandatory
stainChemistry: Nail polish is a tough plastic film with concentrated pigment, designed to dry into a hard layer that doesn't come off easily. The chemistry that takes polish off — acetone, ester solvents — is exactly what risks damaging polyester. Pure 100% polyester usually tolerates acetone and non-acetone removers, but polyester blends commonly contain acetate, which acetone DESTROYS — acetate fibers melt on contact. Most polyester garments don't disclose blend percentages, so a hidden-area test is mandatory before any solvent. Non-acetone polish remover is the safer first try because it's mostly ethyl acetate and other esters with less risk to acetate components. The recurring traps: acetone on a polyester-acetate blend (irreversible damage), heat (sets pigment into polyester permanently), and rubbing (smears the polish into a wider area).
whyThisWorks: Hidden-area test screens for acetate content (which would bleed or melt under acetone). Once tested, non-acetone polish remover (mostly ethyl acetate) softens the polish film without the acetate-destruction risk acetone has. Pure acetone is the harder-hitting option for stubborn polish on confirmed pure polyester only. Cloth tamping from the back with a transfer pad pushes polish out instead of deeper. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing. The honest cap: aged polish on dyed polyester often plateaus with a faint pigment shadow.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): polish is nitrocellulose film former plus plasticizers plus pigment in ester solvents (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate, sometimes acetone). Acetone dissolves cellulose acetate fibers irreversibly. Polyester (PET) is solvent-resistant. Pro uses amyl acetate at controlled concentration plus tight blotting plus NSD rinse plus acetic acid neutralization.
defaultAssumption: Nail polish drip on a polyester garment, fresh and not yet hot-dried. Pure 100% polyester = home protocol candidate with hidden test. Polyester blend with unknown content = pro is the right call. Hot-dried polish = often permanent.

spottingProtocol.agent: Lift Excess Wet Polish — Plastic Edge
spottingProtocol.instruction: If polish is still wet: use the FLAT edge of a plastic card to lift bulk wet polish off the surface. Don't smear or rub. Lift, place on cloth, wipe card. If polish is dry: leave the dry film alone — solvent dissolves it later.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Non-acetone polish remover on Q-tip, acetone on Q-tip
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mandatory before any solvent. Inside seam: dab non-acetone remover, blot with white cloth. Color or finish transfer = skip remover entirely. Then test acetone on a SEPARATE hidden seam: dab, blot. Fiber softens or fabric loses sheen = polyester contains acetate, ACETONE BANNED. Both clean = pure polyester, acetone option available.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Stack of clean white paper towels under the spot
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place a thick stack of clean white paper towels UNDER the stained area, polish-side facing the cloth. Work from the BACK. Polish needs somewhere to go — the cloth underneath catches the transfer.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Non-acetone nail polish remover on white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Start with non-acetone remover (safer if blend has any acetate content). Apply to a clean white cloth — never pour on the garment. Tamp the BACK of the spot. Watch the cloth underneath — replace it as it picks up color. 2–3 passes. STOP if the spot starts SPREADING outward — that's a fiber-bleed signal.

spottingProtocol.agent: Acetone — Pure Polyester Only, If Non-Acetone Plateaus
spottingProtocol.equipment: 100% acetone on white cotton cloth, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: ONLY if hidden test confirmed pure polyester (no acetate bleed) AND non-acetone plateaued: 100% acetone on a white cloth, tamp from the back, 30–60 sec passes. Replace cloth often. STOP if spreading. Open windows; flammable, no flame. Acetate-blend polyester = SKIP, pro from there.

Dawn worked into a damp cloth, press the spot 5 min to clear solvent residue. Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry — wet polyester can hide residual pigment that reappears once dry.
AIR-DRY only. Inspect in daylight. Faint pigment shadow remaining = honest cap on dyed polyester; further passes risk fiber damage. Stop, accept, or escalate to pro.

steps: 1. Lift bulk wet polish with plastic-edge — no smearing.
2. Hidden-area test for acetate content (non-acetone + acetone separately).
3. Back-side cloth setup as transfer pad.
5. Acetone only on pure polyester if non-acetone plateaus.
7. AIR-DRY + daylight inspect — accept honest cap or escalate.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets pigment into polyester
warnings: Hidden-area test for acetate content is mandatory — acetone destroys acetate fibers irreversibly
Most polyester garments don't disclose blend percentages — assume blend until tested
STOP if spot is SPREADING during solvent contact — fiber bleed
NEVER hot water or hot dryer until cleared
Always blot, never rub — rubbing smears polish
Work from BACK with cloth underneath — pushes polish OUT
Acetone is flammable — no flame, ventilate
Honest cap: aged polish on dyed polyester often plateaus

homeSolutions.agent: Acetone on Polyester Is Bounded — Hidden Test First
homeSolutions.instruction: Polyester usually tolerates acetone, BUT polyester blends commonly contain acetate, and acetone DESTROYS acetate fibers — they melt on contact. Most polyester garments don't say 'I have acetate in me' on the tag. The hidden-area test before any solvent is mandatory. Two hard rules: (1) hidden test BEFORE acetone, and (2) NO hot water or hot dryer until cleared. Plus: stop if the spot starts spreading — that's a fiber-bleed signal.

homeSolutions.agent: Lift Wet Polish With a Plastic Card — Don't Smear
homeSolutions.instruction: If the polish is still wet: use the FLAT edge of a plastic card (gift card, credit card) to lift the bulk off the surface. Don't smear, rub, or wipe — that pushes polish into the fiber. Lift, place on cloth, wipe card. If the polish has dried, leave the dry film alone — solvent dissolves it later.

homeSolutions.agent: Hidden-Area Test — TWO Tests, Separate Spots
homeSolutions.instruction: On an inside seam, dab a drop of non-acetone polish remover, blot with a white cloth. Color or finish transfer = skip remover, go to pro. On a SEPARATE inside seam, dab a drop of pure acetone, blot. If the fabric softens, sheen disappears, or the fiber feels weird, your polyester has acetate in it — ACETONE BANNED. Both clean = pure polyester, acetone option is available later. If you skip this test and use acetone on an acetate blend, the damage is permanent.

homeSolutions.agent: Set Up Cloth Underneath — Transfer Pad
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a thick stack of clean white paper towels UNDERNEATH the stained area. Work from the BACK of the garment, polish-side facing the cloth. Polish needs somewhere to go — the cloth underneath catches the transfer.

Start with non-acetone polish remover (safer if there's any acetate content). Apply to a clean white cloth — never pour on the garment. Tamp the BACK of the spot, replace the underneath cloth as it picks up color. 2 to 3 passes. STOP if the spot starts SPREADING outward — fiber-bleed signal.

homeSolutions.agent: Acetone — Only on Confirmed Pure Polyester
homeSolutions.instruction: ONLY if the hidden-area test confirmed pure polyester (no acetate bleed) AND non-acetone plateaued: 100% pure acetone on a white cloth, tamp from the back, 30 to 60 seconds per pass. Replace the cloth often as it picks up color. STOP if spreading. Open windows — acetone is flammable, no flame nearby. If the test showed acetate content, SKIP this step entirely — pro from there.
Dawn worked into a damp cloth, press the spot for 5 minutes to clear solvent residue. Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY only. Inspect in daylight before the dryer.

homeSolutions.agent: Honest Cap — Faint Pigment Shadow Often Remains
homeSolutions.instruction: Aged polish on dyed polyester often plateaus at 70% to 80% cleared with a faint pigment shadow. If you've hit that point after 4 to 5 passes, more passes risk fiber damage. Stop, accept, or escalate to pro for valuable garments.

steps: 1. Hidden-area test FIRST for acetate content (non-acetone + acetone separately).
2. NO hot water. NO hot dryer until cleared.
3. Lift wet polish with plastic-edge — no smearing.
4. Stack of paper towels UNDER the spot — transfer pad.
5. Non-acetone remover cloth tamp from BACK first.
6. Acetone cloth tamp ONLY on pure polyester if non-acetone plateaus.
7. STOP if spot is SPREADING — pro from there.
8. Dawn damp cloth + cool machine wash.
9. AIR-DRY + daylight inspect — accept honest cap or escalate.
products: 100% Acetone (pure polyester only, hidden test required)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hidden-area test shows acetate content (acetone BANNED, pro for stubborn polish). Stop if spot is SPREADING during solvent contact. Stop on hot-dried polish (often permanent). Stop on dry-clean-only / specialty polyester (pro). Stop after 4–5 passes plateau — accept honest cap.
warnings: Hidden-area test for acetate content is MANDATORY — acetone destroys acetate fibers irreversibly
Most polyester garments don't disclose blend percentages — assume blend until tested
NEVER hot water or hot dryer until cleared
Always blot, never rub — rubbing smears polish
Work from BACK with cloth underneath
Acetone is flammable — no flame, ventilate
Honest cap: aged polish on dyed polyester often plateaus

neverDo: Never use acetone on polyester without hidden test — acetate blends destroyed irreversibly
Never hot water or hot dryer on nail polish + polyester — sets pigment
Never rub — smears polish into a wider area
Never continue if spot is SPREADING — fiber bleed
fiberSensitivities: Pure 100% polyester (PET): tolerates acetone after hidden test
Polyester-acetate blend (common in dressy polyester): acetone DESTROYS acetate fibers — non-acetone only, often pro
Polyester-spandex / Lycra blend: acetate may be in the spandex coating — hidden test mandatory
Performance / specialty polyester (compression, technical): pro — coatings react unpredictably
Honest cap on dyed polyester for aged polish — partial removal is the norm

materialWarnings: Hidden-area test for acetate content is MANDATORY before acetone
Polyester-acetate blends are common — acetone destroys them irreversibly
Stop if spreading — fiber bleed signal
No hot water / no hot dryer — sets pigment
Honest cap: aged polish on dyed polyester often plateaus

escalation.when: Hidden-area test shows acetate content (acetone BANNED). Spot is SPREADING during solvent contact. Hot-dried polish (often permanent). Dry-clean-only / specialty / performance polyester. 4–5 pass plateau on dyed polyester. Acetone was used without hidden test (acetate damage assessment).
escalation.whatToTell: "Nail polish on [pure 100% / blend / acetate-blend / athletic / specialty] polyester, [color], age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [plastic-edge lift + non-acetone remover cloth tamp + acetone (if pure polyester) + Dawn + cool wash]. No hot. [Hidden test result / spreading observed / acetate damage suspected / honest cap reached]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting capability — they have stronger controlled solvents (amyl acetate at controlled concentration) home users can't safely match

contextQuestions: Pure 100% polyester or blend (acetate, spandex, Lycra)?
Standard polish, gel polish, or quick-dry formula?
Fresh, hours old, or hot-dried already?
Has acetone been used without hidden test?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The acetate-blend risk is the central polyester polish challenge — modern dressy polyester (especially shirts and dresses) often contains acetate for drape, and most garments don't disclose the percentage. Hidden-area test is the only way to know. Gel polish is harder than standard polish because of the photo-cured polymer; pro is often the right call. Quick-dry formulas have more solvent and dry harder. Honest cap applies on dyed polyester regardless.
